    Database Migrations

    When you're working with databases for the first time, the process of managing changes can feel overwhelming. But understanding how databases, schemas, and migrations work together is crucial to building real applications.

    In this lesson, you'll learn how to manage database changes using a three-step process: define your schema, generate migrations, and apply those migrations to your database.

    The Database Files

    When you run the seed script, SQLite creates files on your file system that represent your entire database. Unlike databases like Postgres that run on remote servers, SQLite lets you see and even delete these files directly.

    If you ever need to reset your database completely, you can simply delete the data.db file and re-run the seed script.

    The Three-Step Process

    Managing database changes follows this pattern:

    1. Edit your schema in schema.ts
    2. Run pnpm db:generate to create migration files
    3. Run pnpm db:migrate to apply those migrations to your database

    This process keeps your database synchronized with your code.

    Steps To Complete

    • Open your package.json file and locate the db scripts

    Look for scripts like db:seed, db:generate, and db:migrate. These are the tools you'll use to manage your database.

    • Delete the data.db file from your project

    This simulates needing to reset your database. You can do this through your file explorer or terminal.

    • Run the dev server and observe the error
    pnpm dev

    Visit localhost:5175 in your browser. You should see an error about a missing table. This happens because your database no longer exists.

    • Stop the dev server and re-seed your database
    pnpm db:seed

    Check your file system. The data.db file should have reappeared.

    • Open the schema.ts file and examine the table definitions

    Look at tables like users, categories, courses, modules, and lessons. Don't worry about understanding every detail, just get familiar with how the schema defines your database structure.

    • Make a small change to one of the tables in your schema

    Add a new column or modify an existing one. This simulates making a real database change.

    • Run the generate command
    pnpm db:generate

    Check the file system for new migration files. They should appear in a migrations folder.

    • Run the migrate command
    pnpm db:migrate

    This applies your schema changes to the actual database file.

    • Verify the changes took effect by checking your database

    You can use a SQLite viewer or inspect the data.db file to confirm the migration ran successfully.

    • Practice resetting everything

    Delete the data.db file, run pnpm db:seed, and confirm everything works again. This gives you confidence that you understand the reset process when you need it later.
